num_hidden_layers.description:
Controls the depth of the neural network. Increasing this generally leads
to better accuracy from the increased descriptivity, but comes at the cost
of increased training and evaluation time.
num_hidden_layers.title: Num Hidden Layers

num_neurons_per_layer.description:
Controls the width of the neural network. Increasing this generally leads
to better accuracy from the increased descriptivity, but comes at the cost
of increased training and evaluation time.
num_neurons_per_layer.title: Num Neurons Per Layer

layernorm.description:
Whether to use layer normalization before the neural network. Setting this
hyperparameter to false will lead to slower convergence of training, but
might lead to better generalization outside of the training set distribution.
layernorm.title: Layernorm

CartesianTargetTypeHypers.description: Hyperparameters to specify cartesian target types.
CartesianTargetTypeHypers.title: CartesianTargetTypeHypers

DescriptorHypers.description: Descriptor hyperparameters wrapping the RepFlow block.

type.description: Descriptor type identifier used by deepmd-kit.
type.title: Type

activation_function.description:
Activation function. Format: ``"name"`` or ``"name:param"``.
Supported names include ``"tanh"``, ``"gelu"``, ``"custom_silu"``.
activation_function.title: Activation Function

use_tebd_bias.description: Add bias to the type embedding.
use_tebd_bias.title: Use Tebd Bias

precision.description:
Floating-point precision for the descriptor (``32`` or ``64``).
This controls the internal precision of deepmd-kit's descriptor
computation.  For mixed-precision training, set this independently
of ``fitting_net.precision``; for uniform precision, set both to the
same value and match ``base_precision`` accordingly.
precision.title: Precision

concat_output_tebd.description: Concatenate type embedding to descriptor output.
concat_output_tebd.title: Concat Output Tebd

DescriptorHypers.title: DescriptorHypers

neuron.description: Hidden layer sizes for the fitting network.
neuron.title: Neuron

resnet_dt.description: Use a ResNet-style time step in each hidden layer.
resnet_dt.title: Resnet Dt

precision.description:
Floating-point precision for the fitting network (``32`` or ``64``).
Can differ from ``descriptor.precision`` for mixed-precision training.
precision.title: Precision

activation_function.description: Activation function (same format as the descriptor).
activation_function.title: Activation Function

trainable.description: Whether fitting network weights are trainable.
trainable.title: Trainable

rcond.description: Cutoff for pseudo-inverse in linear fitting.
rcond.title: Rcond

use_aparam_as_mask.description: Treat atom-level parameters as a mask.
use_aparam_as_mask.title: Use Aparam As Mask

FullFinetuneHypers.description:
Hyperparameters to use full finetuning of PET models.

This means all model parameters are trainable.

read_from.description: Path to the pretrained model checkpoint.
read_from.title: Read From

config.description: No configuration needed for full finetuning.
config.title: Config

inherit_heads.description:
Mapping from new trainable targets (keys) to the existing targets
in the model (values).
This allows for copying weights from the corresponding
source heads to the destination heads instead of random initialization.
inherit_heads.title: Inherit Heads

FullFinetuneHypers.title: FullFinetuneHypers

head_modules.description: List of module name prefixes for the prediction heads to finetune.
head_modules.title: Head Modules

last_layer_modules.description: List of module name prefixes for the last layers to finetune.
last_layer_modules.title: Last Layer Modules

HeadsFinetuneHypers.description:
Hyperparameters for heads finetuning of PET models.

Freezes all model parameters except for the prediction heads
and last layers.

read_from.description: Path to the pretrained model checkpoint.
read_from.title: Read From

inherit_heads.description:
Mapping from new trainable targets (keys) to the existing targets
in the model (values).
This allows for copying weights from the corresponding
source heads to the destination heads instead of random initialization.
inherit_heads.title: Inherit Heads

HeadsFinetuneHypers.title: HeadsFinetuneHypers

IndicesOnlyHypers.description: Config for validation/test sets that reference the training source via indices.
IndicesOnlyHypers.title: IndicesOnlyHypers

degree.description: Degree of the kernel. For now, only 2 is allowed.
degree.title: Degree

num_sparse_points.description:
Number of sparse points to use during
the training, it select the number of actual samples
to use during the training. The selection is done with
the Further Point Sampling (FPS) algorithm.
The optimal number of sparse points depends on the system.
Increasing it might impreve the accuracy, but it also increase the
memory and time required for training.
num_sparse_points.title: Num Sparse Points

alpha.description: Scaling factor for the LoRA matrices.
alpha.title: Alpha

LoRaFinetuneHypers.description:
Hyperparameters for LoRA finetuning of PET models.

Injects LoRA layers and finetunes only them.

read_from.description: Path to the pretrained model checkpoint.
read_from.title: Read From

inherit_heads.description:
Mapping from new trainable targets (keys) to the existing targets
in the model (values).
This allows for copying weights from the corresponding
source heads to the destination heads instead of random initialization.
inherit_heads.title: Inherit Heads

LoRaFinetuneHypers.title: LoRaFinetuneHypers

LongRangeHypers.description:
In some systems and datasets, enabling long-range Coulomb interactions
might be beneficial for the accuracy of the model and/or
its physical correctness.

use_ewald.description: Use Ewald summation. If False, P3M is used
use_ewald.title: Use Ewald

kspace_resolution.description: Resolution of the reciprocal space grid
kspace_resolution.title: Kspace Resolution

interpolation_nodes.description: Number of grid points for interpolation (for PME only)
interpolation_nodes.title: Interpolation Nodes

LongRangeHypers.title: LongRangeHypers

NoFinetuneHypers.description: Hypers that indicate that no finetuning is to be applied.

read_from.description:
No finetuning is indicated by setting this argument to None.

The rest of finetuning hyperparameters are then ignored.
read_from.title: Read From

NoFinetuneHypers.title: NoFinetuneHypers

RadialBasisHypers.description: Hyperparameter concerning the radial basis functions used in the model.

max_eigenvalue.description: Maximum eigenvalue for the radial basis.
max_eigenvalue.title: Max Eigenvalue

element_scale.description: Scaling factor for the element-dependent radial lengthscales.
element_scale.title: Element Scale

mlp_depth.description: Depth of the radial MLP. Must be at least 2.
mlp_depth.title: Mlp Depth

mlp_expansion_ratio.description: Expansion ratio for the radial MLP hidden layers.
mlp_expansion_ratio.title: Mlp Expansion Ratio

e_rcut.description: Edge (pair) cutoff radius in length units.
e_rcut.title: E Rcut

e_rcut_smth.description: Start of cosine smoothing for the edge cutoff.
e_rcut_smth.title: E Rcut Smth

e_sel.description: Maximum number of edge neighbors per atom.
e_sel.title: E Sel

a_rcut.description: Angle (triplet) cutoff radius in length units.

a_rcut_smth.description: Start of cosine smoothing for the angle cutoff.

a_sel.description: Maximum number of angle neighbors per atom.

axis_neuron.description: Number of axis neurons in the embedding network.
axis_neuron.title: Axis Neuron

a_compress_use_split.description: Use split compression for angle features.
a_compress_use_split.title: A Compress Use Split

update_angle.description: Update angle features at each layer.
update_angle.title: Update Angle

update_residual_init.description:
Initialisation for the residual scaling. Options: ``"const"``,
``"norm"``.
update_residual_init.title: Update Residual Init

smooth_edge_update.description: Apply smooth cutoff function to edge updates.
smooth_edge_update.title: Smooth Edge Update

use_dynamic_sel.description: Dynamically adjust neighbor selection at runtime.
use_dynamic_sel.title: Use Dynamic Sel

sel_reduce_factor.description: Reduction factor for dynamic neighbor selection.
sel_reduce_factor.title: Sel Reduce Factor

SOAPBasisHypers.description: Basis configuration for the SOAP descriptor.

max_angular.description:
Maximum angular channels of the spherical harmonics when
computing the SOAP descriptors. In general, increasing this
hyperparameter might lead to better accuracy, especially on larger
datasets, at the cost of increased training and evaluation time.
max_angular.title: Max Angular

SOAPBasisHypers.title: SOAPBasisHypers

SOAPBasisRadialHypers.description: Radial basis configuration for the SOAP descriptor.

max_radial.description:
Maximum radial channels of the spherical harmonics when
computing the SOAP descriptors. In general, increasing this
hyperparameter might lead to better accuracy, especially on larger
datasets, at the cost of increased training and evaluation time.
max_radial.title: Max Radial

SOAPBasisRadialHypers.title: SOAPBasisRadialHypers

max_angular.description:
Maximum angular channels of the spherical harmonics when
computing the SOAP descriptors.
max_angular.title: Max Angular

max_radial.description:
Maximum radial channels of the spherical harmonics when
computing the SOAP descriptors.
max_radial.title: Max Radial

cutoff.description: Determines the cutoff routine of the atomic environment.

SOAPCutoffConfig.description: Cutoff configuration for the SOAP descriptor.

radius.description:
Should be set to a value after which most of interatomic is expected
to be negligible. Note that the values should be defined in the position
units of your dataset.
radius.title: Radius

width.description:
The radial cutoff of atomic environments is performed smoothly, over
another distance defined by this parameter.
width.title: Width

SOAPCutoffConfig.title: SOAPCutoffConfig

SOAPCutoffHypers.description: Cutoff configuration for the SOAP descriptor.

radius.description:
This should be set to a value after which most of the
interactions between atoms is expected to be negligible.
radius.title: Radius

SOAPCutoffHypers.title: SOAPCutoffHypers

SOAPCutoffSmoothingHypers.description: Smoothing configuration for the SOAP descriptor cutoff.

type.description:
Cutoff function used to smooth the behavior around the cutoff
radius.

Currently, only the Shifted Cosine function is supported, which is defined
as ``f(r) = 1/2 * (1 + cos(π (r- cutoff + width) / width ))``.
type.title: Type

SOAPCutoffSmoothingHypers.title: SOAPCutoffSmoothingHypers

SOAPDensityHypers.description: Density configuration for the SOAP descriptor.

type.description:
Currently, we only support a Gaussian type orbitals (GTO) as radial basis
functions and radial integrals.
type.title: Type

center_atom_weight.description:
Weight of the central atom contribution to the features. If
1.0 the center atom contribution is weighted the same as any other
contribution. If 0.0 the central atom does not contribute to the features
at all.
center_atom_weight.title: Center Atom Weight

width.description:
Width of the atom-centered gaussian creating the atomic
density.
width.title: Width

scaling.description:
Radial scaling can be used to reduce the importance of neighbor
atoms further away from the center, usually improving the performance
of the model.

SOAPDensityHypers.title: SOAPDensityHypers

SOAPDensityScalingHypers.description: Density scaling configuration for the SOAP descriptor.

type.description:
Type of scaling, currently only one type is supported. It uses a
long-range algebraic decay and smooth behavior at
:math:`r\rightarrow 0`: as introduced by :footcite:t:`willatt_feature_2018` as
``f(r) = rate / (rate + (r / scale) ^ exponent)``.
type.title: Type

SOAPDensityScalingHypers.title: SOAPDensityScalingHypers

cutoff.description: Spherical cutoff (Å) to use for atomic environments.

SphericalTargetTypeHypers.description: Hyperparameters to specify spherical target types.
SphericalTargetTypeHypers.title: SphericalTargetTypeHypers

SystemsHypers.description: Hyperparameters for the systems in the dataset.
SystemsHypers.title: SystemsHypers

TargetHypers.description: Hyperparameters for the targets in the dataset.
TargetHypers.title: TargetHypers

name.description: Name of the architecture. The architecure options will depend on the chosen architecture.
name.title: Name

name.description: Name of the architecture. The architecure options will depend on the chosen architecture.
name.title: Name

model.description:
Hyperparameters for the DPA3 model.

The ``type_map`` needed by deepmd-kit is derived automatically from the
dataset's atomic numbers and should not be set manually.

name.description: Name of the architecture. The architecure options will depend on the chosen architecture.
name.title: Name

name.description: Name of the architecture. The architecure options will depend on the chosen architecture.
name.title: Name

name.description: Name of the architecture. The architecure options will depend on the chosen architecture.
name.title: Name

name.description: Name of the architecture. The architecure options will depend on the chosen architecture.
name.title: Name

name.description: Name of the architecture. The architecure options will depend on the chosen architecture.
name.title: Name

hidden_sizes.description:
List of hidden layer sizes for the MLP.
For example, [64, 32] creates a 2-layer MLP with 64 and 32 neurons
respectively. The last hidden size should be set to a small number
(generally one or two) if the goal is to extract collective variables.
hidden_sizes.title: Hidden Sizes

feature_layer_index.description:
Index of the MLP layer to be mapped to the 'feature' output.
Can be negative to index from the end. Default is -1 (the output layer).
feature_layer_index.title: Feature Layer Index

warmup_fraction.description:
Fraction of total training steps used for learning rate warmup.
The learning rate increases linearly from 0 to the base learning rate
during this period, then follows a cosine annealing schedule.
warmup_fraction.title: Warmup Fraction

model_checkpoint.description:
Path to the pre-trained model checkpoint.
This checkpoint's backbone will be frozen and used for feature extraction.
model_checkpoint.title: Model Checkpoint

log_interval.description: Interval for logging training progress (in epochs).
log_interval.title: Log Interval

checkpoint_interval.description: Interval for saving checkpoints during training (in epochs).
checkpoint_interval.title: Checkpoint Interval

metatrain__experimental__dpa3__documenta.description:
Hyperparameters for the DPA3 model.

The ``type_map`` needed by deepmd-kit is derived automatically from the
dataset's atomic numbers and should not be set manually.

dpa3_model.description:
Path to a pretrained DPA3 model file (deepmd-kit checkpoint or saved
Module).  When provided, the model weights are loaded from this file
instead of being initialised from scratch.  Energy biases and standard
deviations stored in the deepmd-kit model are extracted and handed to
metatrain's ``CompositionModel`` and ``Scaler`` so that fine-tuning starts
from the pretrained values.
dpa3_model.title: Dpa3 Model

descriptor.description: Descriptor configuration (RepFlow block and related settings).

metatrain__experimental__dpa3__documenta.title: ModelHypers

batch_size.description:
The number of samples to use in each batch of training. This
hyperparameter controls the tradeoff between training speed and memory usage. In
general, larger batch sizes will lead to faster training, but might require more
memory.
batch_size.title: Batch Size

scheduler_patience.description: Number of epochs with no improvement before reducing the learning rate.
scheduler_patience.title: Scheduler Patience

scheduler_factor.description: Factor by which the learning rate is reduced on plateau.
scheduler_factor.title: Scheduler Factor

scale_targets.description:
Normalize targets to unit std during training.

If true, a single scale is computed for each target, given by the uncentered
standard deviation across all values in the dataset for that target.

For targets with more than one property (i.e. > 1 block or >= 1 block with > 1
property), per-property scales are also computed, and used to re-scale model
predictions.

See also :ref:`scale-targets`.
scale_targets.title: Scale Targets

fixed_composition_weights.description:
Weights for atomic contributions.

This is passed to the ``fixed_weights`` argument of
:meth:`CompositionModel.train_model
<metatrain.utils.additive.composition.CompositionModel.train_model>`,
see its documentation to understand exactly what to pass here.
fixed_composition_weights.title: Fixed Composition Weights

best_model_metric.description: Metric used to select best checkpoint (e.g., ``rmse_prod``).
best_model_metric.title: Best Model Metric

loss.description:
This section describes the loss function to be used. See the
:ref:`loss-functions` for more details.
loss.title: Loss

predict_momenta_as_difference.description:
This parameter controls whether the model will
predict future momenta directly or as a difference between the future and the
current momenta. Setting it to true will help when predicting relatively small
timesteps (when compared to the momentum autocorrelation time), while setting
it to false is beneficial when predicting large timesteps.
predict_momenta_as_difference.title: Predict Momenta As Difference

cutoff.description:
Cutoff radius for neighbor search.

This should be set to a value after which most of the interactions
between atoms is expected to be negligible. A lower cutoff will lead
to faster models.
cutoff.title: Cutoff

cutoff_width.description: Width of the smoothing function at the cutoff
cutoff_width.title: Cutoff Width

d_pet.description:
Dimension of the edge features.

This hyperparameters controls width of the neural network. In general,
increasing it might lead to better accuracy, especially on larger datasets, at the
cost of increased training and evaluation time.
d_pet.title: D Pet

d_node.description:
Dimension of the node features.

Increasing this hyperparameter might lead to better accuracy,
with a relatively small increase in inference time.
d_node.title: D Node

d_feedforward.description: Dimension of the feedforward network in the attention layer.
d_feedforward.title: D Feedforward

num_attention_layers.description:
The number of attention layers in each layer of the graph
neural network. Depending on the dataset, increasing this hyperparameter might
lead to better accuracy, at the cost of increased training and evaluation time.
num_attention_layers.title: Num Attention Layers

num_gnn_layers.description:
The number of graph neural network layers.

In general, decreasing this hyperparameter to 1 will lead to much faster models,
at the expense of accuracy. Increasing it may or may not lead to better accuracy,
depending on the dataset, at the cost of increased training and evaluation time.
num_gnn_layers.title: Num Gnn Layers

attention_temperature.description: The temperature scaling factor for attention scores.
attention_temperature.title: Attention Temperature

transformer_type.description:
The order in which the layer normalization and attention
are applied in a transformer block. Available options are ``PreLN``
(normalization before attention) and ``PostLN`` (normalization after attention).
transformer_type.title: Transformer Type

featurizer_type.description:
Implementation of the featurizer of the model to use. Available
options are ``residual`` (the original featurizer from the PET paper, that uses
residual connections at each GNN layer for readout) and ``feedforward`` (a modern
version that uses the last representation after all GNN iterations for readout).
Additionally, the feedforward version uses bidirectional features flow during the
message passing iterations, that favors features flowing from atom ``i`` to atom
``j`` to be not equal to the features flowing from atom ``j`` to atom ``i``.
featurizer_type.title: Featurizer Type

timestep.description:
The time interval (in fs) between the current and the future positions
and momenta that the model must predict. This option is not used in the
training, but it is registered in the model and it will be used to validate
that the timestep used during inference in MD engines is the same as the
one used during training. This hyperparameter must be provided by the user.
timestep.title: Timestep

masses.description:
A dictionary mapping atomic species to their masses (in atomic mass
units).

Indeed, it should be noted that FlashMD models, as implemented in metatrain,
are not transferable across different isotopes. The masses are not used during
training, but they are registered in the model and they will be used during
inference to validate that the masses used in MD engines are the same as the
ones used during training. If not provided, masses from the ``ase.data``
module will be used. These correspond to masses averaged over the natural
isotopic abundance of each element.
masses.title: Masses

batch_size.description:
The number of samples to use in each batch of training. This
hyperparameter controls the tradeoff between training speed and memory usage. In
general, larger batch sizes will lead to faster training, but might require more
memory.
batch_size.title: Batch Size

warmup_fraction.description: Fraction of training steps used for learning rate warmup.
warmup_fraction.title: Warmup Fraction

atomic_baseline.description:
The baselines for each target.

By default, ``metatrain`` will fit a linear model (:class:`CompositionModel
<metatrain.utils.additive.composition.CompositionModel>`) to compute the
least squares baseline for each atomic species for each target.

However, this hyperparameter allows you to provide your own baselines.
The value of the hyperparameter should be a dictionary where the keys are the
target names, and the values are either (1) a single baseline to be used for
all atomic types, or (2) a dictionary mapping atomic types to their baselines.
For example:

- ``atomic_baseline: {"energy": {1: -0.5, 6: -10.0}}`` will fix the energy
  baseline for hydrogen (Z=1) to -0.5 and for carbon (Z=6) to -10.0, while
  fitting the baselines for the energy of all other atomic types, as well
  as fitting the baselines for all other targets.
- ``atomic_baseline: {"energy": -5.0}`` will fix the energy baseline for
  all atomic types to -5.0.
- ``atomic_baseline: {"mtt:dos": 0.0}`` sets the baseline for the "mtt:dos"
  target to 0.0, effectively disabling the atomic baseline for that target.

This atomic baseline is substracted from the targets during training, which
avoids the main model needing to learn atomic contributions, and likely makes
training easier. When the model is used in evaluation mode, the atomic baseline
is added on top of the model predictions automatically.

.. note::

    This atomic baseline is a per-atom contribution. Therefore, if the property
    you are predicting is a sum over all atoms (e.g., total energy), the
    contribution of the atomic baseline to the total property will be the
    atomic baseline multiplied by the number of atoms of that type in the
    structure.
atomic_baseline.title: Atomic Baseline

scale_targets.description:
Normalize targets to unit std during training.

If true, a single scale is computed for each target, given by the uncentered
standard deviation across all values in the dataset for that target.

For targets with more than one property (i.e. > 1 block or >= 1 block with > 1
property), per-property scales are also computed, and used to re-scale model
predictions.

See also :ref:`scale-targets`.
scale_targets.title: Scale Targets

fixed_scaling_weights.description:
Weights for target scaling.

This is passed to the ``fixed_weights`` argument of
:meth:`Scaler.train_model <metatrain.utils.scaler.scaler.Scaler.train_model>`,
see its documentation to understand exactly what to pass here.
fixed_scaling_weights.title: Fixed Scaling Weights

per_structure_targets.description: Targets to calculate per-structure losses and errors on.
per_structure_targets.title: Per Structure Targets

num_workers.description:
Number of workers for data loading. If not provided, it is set
automatically.
num_workers.title: Num Workers

best_model_metric.description: Metric used to select best checkpoint (e.g., ``rmse_prod``)
best_model_metric.title: Best Model Metric

grad_clip_norm.description: Maximum gradient norm value, by default inf (no clipping)
grad_clip_norm.title: Grad Clip Norm

loss.description:
This section describes the loss function to be used. See the
:ref:`loss-functions` for more details.
loss.title: Loss

batch_atom_bounds.description:
Bounds for the number of atoms per batch as [min, max]. Batches with atom
counts outside these bounds will be skipped during training. Use ``None`` for
either value to disable that bound. This is useful for preventing out-of-memory
errors and ensuring consistent computational load. Default: ``[None, None]``.
batch_atom_bounds.title: Batch Atom Bounds

finetune.description: Parameters for fine-tuning trained FlashMD models.
finetune.title: Finetune

cutoff.description:
Cutoff radius for neighbor search.

This should be set to a value after which most of the interactions
between atoms is expected to be negligible. A lower cutoff will lead
to faster models.
cutoff.title: Cutoff

cutoff_width.description: Width of the smoothing function at the cutoff
cutoff_width.title: Cutoff Width

d_pet.description:
Dimension of the edge features.

This hyperparameters controls width of the neural network. In general,
increasing it might lead to better accuracy, especially on larger datasets, at the
cost of increased training and evaluation time.
d_pet.title: D Pet

d_node.description:
Dimension of the node features.

Increasing this hyperparameter might lead to better accuracy,
with a relatively small increase in inference time.
d_node.title: D Node

d_feedforward.description: Dimension of the feedforward network in the attention layer.
d_feedforward.title: D Feedforward

num_attention_layers.description:
The number of attention layers in each layer of the graph
neural network. Depending on the dataset, increasing this hyperparameter might
lead to better accuracy, at the cost of increased training and evaluation time.
num_attention_layers.title: Num Attention Layers

num_gnn_layers.description:
The number of graph neural network layers.

In general, decreasing this hyperparameter to 1 will lead to much faster models,
at the expense of accuracy. Increasing it may or may not lead to better accuracy,
depending on the dataset, at the cost of increased training and evaluation time.
num_gnn_layers.title: Num Gnn Layers

attention_temperature.description: The temperature scaling factor for attention scores.
attention_temperature.title: Attention Temperature

transformer_type.description:
The order in which the layer normalization and attention
are applied in a transformer block. Available options are ``PreLN``
(normalization before attention) and ``PostLN`` (normalization after attention).
transformer_type.title: Transformer Type

featurizer_type.description:
Implementation of the featurizer of the model to use. Available
options are ``residual`` (the original featurizer from the PET paper, that uses
residual connections at each GNN layer for readout) and ``feedforward`` (a modern
version that uses the last representation after all GNN iterations for readout).
Additionally, the feedforward version uses bidirectional features flow during the
message passing iterations, that favors features flowing from atom ``i`` to atom
``j`` to be not equal to the features flowing from atom ``j`` to atom ``i``.
featurizer_type.title: Featurizer Type

metatrain__experimental__flashmd_symplec.description: Hyperparameters for training symplectic FlashMD models.

timestep.description:
The time interval (in fs) between the current and the future positions
and momenta that the model must predict. This option is not used in the
training, but it is registered in the model and it will be used to validate
that the timestep used during inference in MD engines is the same as the
one used during training. This hyperparameter must be provided by the user.
timestep.title: Timestep

masses.description:
A dictionary mapping atomic species to their masses (in atomic mass
units).

Indeed, it should be noted that FlashMD models, as implemented in metatrain,
are not transferable across different isotopes. The masses are not used during
training, but they are registered in the model and they will be used during
inference to validate that the masses used in MD engines are the same as the
ones used during training. If not provided, masses from the ``ase.data``
module will be used. These correspond to masses averaged over the natural
isotopic abundance of each element.
masses.title: Masses

batch_size.description:
The number of samples to use in each batch of training. This
hyperparameter controls the tradeoff between training speed and memory usage. In
general, larger batch sizes will lead to faster training, but might require more
memory.
batch_size.title: Batch Size

warmup_fraction.description: Fraction of training steps used for learning rate warmup.
warmup_fraction.title: Warmup Fraction

scale_targets.description:
Normalize targets to unit std during training.

If true, a single scale is computed for each target, given by the uncentered
standard deviation across all values in the dataset for that target.

For targets with more than one property (i.e. > 1 block or >= 1 block with > 1
property), per-property scales are also computed, and used to re-scale model
predictions.

See also :ref:`scale-targets`.
scale_targets.title: Scale Targets

fixed_scaling_weights.description:
Weights for target scaling.

This is passed to the ``fixed_weights`` argument of
:meth:`Scaler.train_model <metatrain.utils.scaler.scaler.Scaler.train_model>`,
see its documentation to understand exactly what to pass here.

.. warning::

    For symplectic FlashMD, the scaling weights for the variables
    (``mtt::delta_q`` and ``mtt::delta_p``) must be identical. This is because
    they are both derivatives of the same generating function :math:`S^3`.
    Scaling them differently would lead to inconsistencies.
fixed_scaling_weights.title: Fixed Scaling Weights

num_workers.description:
Number of workers for data loading. If not provided, it is set
automatically.
num_workers.title: Num Workers

best_model_metric.description: Metric used to select best checkpoint (e.g., ``rmse_prod``)
best_model_metric.title: Best Model Metric

grad_clip_norm.description: Maximum gradient norm value, by default inf (no clipping)
grad_clip_norm.title: Grad Clip Norm

loss.description:
This section describes the loss function to be used. See the
:ref:`loss-functions` for more details.
loss.title: Loss

batch_atom_bounds.description:
Bounds for the number of atoms per batch as [min, max]. Batches with atom
counts outside these bounds will be skipped during training. Use ``None`` for
either value to disable that bound. This is useful for preventing out-of-memory
errors and ensuring consistent computational load. Default: ``[None, None]``.
batch_atom_bounds.title: Batch Atom Bounds

finetune.description: Parameters for fine-tuning trained FlashMD models.
finetune.title: Finetune

metatrain__experimental__flashmd_symplec.title: TrainerHypers

mace_model.description:
Path to a pretrained MACE model file.

For example, this can be `a foundation MACE model
<https://github.com/ACEsuit/mace-foundations>`_. If not provided, a new MACE model
will be initialized from scratch using the rest of hyperparameters of the
architecture.
mace_model.title: Mace Model

mace_head_target.description:
Target to which the MACE head is related.

``metatrain`` adds arbitrary heads on top of MACE to predict arbitrary targets.
However, MACE models have themselves a head. This hyperparameter specifies which
metatrain target corresponds to the MACE head. For this target, no new head will be
added, and the output of MACE's head will be used directly.

.. note::
    MACE models with multiple heads also exist, but ``metatrain`` only supports
    using this hyperparameter to deal with single-head MACE models for now.
mace_head_target.title: Mace Head Target

r_max.description:
Cutoff radius for neighbor search.

This should be set to a value after which most of the interactions between atoms is
expected to be negligible. A lower cutoff will lead to faster models.
r_max.title: R Max

num_radial_basis.description: Number of radial basis functions for the radial embedding.
num_radial_basis.title: Num Radial Basis

radial_type.description: Type of radial basis functions to use in the radial embedding.
radial_type.title: Radial Type

num_cutoff_basis.description: Number of basis functions for smooth cutoff
num_cutoff_basis.title: Num Cutoff Basis

max_ell.description:
Highest :math:`\ell` of spherical harmonics used in the interactions.

Note that this is not the maximum :math:`\ell` in ``hidden_irreps``, since
hidden_irreps can contain :math:`\ell` values as high as ``max_ell*correlation``.
max_ell.title: Max Ell

interaction.description:
Name of interaction block.

Class that will be used to compute interactions between atoms at each layer.
interaction.title: Interaction

num_interactions.description:
Number of message passing steps.

MACE's last message passing step only outputs scalar features, so if you are
training on a target that is not scalar (e.g. a vector or some spherical
tensor with higher order), the effective number of message passing steps for
that target will be ``num_interactions - 1``.
num_interactions.title: Num Interactions

hidden_irreps.description:
Irreps for hidden node features.

This defines the shape of the node features at each layer of the MACE model
(except for the last layer, which only contains scalars). The notation for
the irreps is `e3nn's standard notation <https://docs.e3nn.org/en/stable/api/
o3/o3_irreps.html>`_. Essentially, the irreps string is a sum of terms of the
form ``{multiplicity}x{ell}{parity}``, where ``{multiplicity}`` is the number of
channels with angular momentum ``{ell}`` and parity ``{parity}`` (``e`` for even,
``o`` for odd). For example, ``16x0e + 32x1o`` means that there are 16 scalar
channels (:math:`\ell=0`) and 32 vector channels (:math:`\ell=1`) at each layer.

Increasing the multiplicities makes the network wider, which generally leads to
better accuracy at the cost of increased training and evaluation time.

Increasing the maximum :math:`\ell` included in the irreps allows the network to
capture more complex angular dependencies. However, its effect might be heavily
dependent on your dataset and target. The hidden irreps should include at least
up to the maximum :math:`\ell` of the target you are training on. For example, if
you are training on dipole moments (:math:`\ell=1`), the hidden irreps should
include at least :math:`\ell=1` channels.

.. note::

    At the time of writing, MACE enforces that all channels of ``hidden_irreps``
    should have the same multiplicity.
hidden_irreps.title: Hidden Irreps

apply_cutoff.description: Apply cutoff to the radial basis functions before MLP
apply_cutoff.title: Apply Cutoff

pair_repulsion.description: Use pair repulsion term with ZBL potential
pair_repulsion.title: Pair Repulsion

distance_transform.description: Use distance transform for radial basis functions
distance_transform.title: Distance Transform

correlation.description:
Correlation order at each layer.

After computing pair-wise (2-body) messages between atoms, MACE applies
products that construct higher order correlations between messages.
This hyperparameter controls the amount of products applied. For example,
``correlation=1`` means that the interactions are purely 2-body, while
``correlation=2`` would roughly equate to including 3-body interactions,
and so on.

This hyperparameter together with ``max_ell`` determine the maximum
angular momentum that will be non-zero in ``hidden_irreps``, which is
``max_ell * correlation``.
correlation.title: Correlation

gate.description:
Non linearity used for the non linear readouts.

This determines which kind of non-linearity is applied in the non linear
readouts. The non linear readouts are: MACE's internal MLP readout (applied
only at the last layer) and arbitrary MLP heads added on top of MACE by
``metatrain``.

The non-linearity is applied only to scalar channels, therefore it won't
have any effect for non-scalar targets.
gate.title: Gate

interaction_first.description:
Name of interaction block for the first interaction layer.

Class that will be used to compute interactions between atoms at the
first layer.
interaction_first.title: Interaction First

MLP_irreps.description:
Hidden irreps of the MLP readouts.

The MLP readouts are: MACE's internal MLP readout (applied
only at the last layer) and arbitrary MLP heads added on top of MACE by
``metatrain``.

The non-linearity is applied only to scalar channels, therefore these
irreps should only contain scalar channels.
MLP_irreps.title: Mlp Irreps

radial_MLP.description:
Width of the radial MLP.

Only used for MACE's internal MLP.
radial_MLP.title: Radial Mlp

use_embedding_readout.description: Use embedding readout for the final output
use_embedding_readout.title: Use Embedding Readout

use_last_readout_only.description:
Use only the last readout for the final output.

This is only used by the internal MACE readout, arbitrary heads by
``metatrain`` always use as input a concatenation of the node
features from all layers.
use_last_readout_only.title: Use Last Readout Only

lr_scheduler_gamma.description: Gamma parameter for learning rate scheduler.
lr_scheduler_gamma.title: Lr Scheduler Gamma

batch_size.description:
The number of samples to use in each batch of training. This
hyperparameter controls the tradeoff between training speed and memory usage. In
general, larger batch sizes will lead to faster training, but might require more
memory.
batch_size.title: Batch Size

scale_targets.description:
Normalize targets to unit std during training.

If true, a single scale is computed for each target, given by the uncentered
standard deviation across all values in the dataset for that target.

For targets with more than one property (i.e. > 1 block or >= 1 block with > 1
property), per-property scales are also computed, and used to re-scale model
predictions.

See also :ref:`scale-targets`.
scale_targets.title: Scale Targets

atomic_baseline.description:
The baselines for each target.

By default, ``metatrain`` will fit a linear model (:class:`CompositionModel
<metatrain.utils.additive.composition.CompositionModel>`) to compute the
least squares baseline for each atomic species for each target.

However, this hyperparameter allows you to provide your own baselines.
The value of the hyperparameter should be a dictionary where the keys are the
target names, and the values are either (1) a single baseline to be used for
all atomic types, or (2) a dictionary mapping atomic types to their baselines.
For example:

- ``atomic_baseline: {"energy": {1: -0.5, 6: -10.0}}`` will fix the energy
  baseline for hydrogen (Z=1) to -0.5 and for carbon (Z=6) to -10.0, while
  fitting the baselines for the energy of all other atomic types, as well
  as fitting the baselines for all other targets.
- ``atomic_baseline: {"energy": -5.0}`` will fix the energy baseline for
  all atomic types to -5.0.
- ``atomic_baseline: {"mtt:dos": 0.0}`` sets the baseline for the "mtt:dos"
  target to 0.0, effectively disabling the atomic baseline for that target.

This atomic baseline is substracted from the targets during training, which
avoids the main model needing to learn atomic contributions, and likely makes
training easier. When the model is used in evaluation mode, the atomic baseline
is added on top of the model predictions automatically.

.. note::
    This atomic baseline is a per-atom contribution. Therefore, if the property
    you are predicting is a sum over all atoms (e.g., total energy), the
    contribution of the atomic baseline to the total property will be the
    atomic baseline multiplied by the number of atoms of that type in the
    structure.

.. note::
    If a MACE model is loaded through the ``mace_model`` hyperparameter, the
    atomic baselines in the MACE model are used by default for the target
    indicated in ``mace_head_target``. If you want to override them, you need
    to set explicitly the baselines for that target in this hyperparameter.
atomic_baseline.title: Atomic Baseline

fixed_scaling_weights.description:
Weights for target scaling.

This is passed to the ``fixed_weights`` argument of :meth:`Scaler.train_model
<metatrain.utils.scaler.scaler.Scaler.train_model>`, see its documentation to
understand exactly what to pass here.

.. note::
    If a MACE model is loaded through the ``mace_model`` hyperparameter, the
    scales in the MACE model are used by default for the target
    indicated in ``mace_head_target``. If you want to override them, you need
    to set explicitly the baselines for that target in this hyperparameter.
fixed_scaling_weights.title: Fixed Scaling Weights

per_structure_targets.description: Targets to calculate per-structure losses and errors on.
per_structure_targets.title: Per Structure Targets

num_workers.description: Number of workers for data loading. If not provided, it is set automatically.
num_workers.title: Num Workers

best_model_metric.description: Metric used to select best checkpoint (e.g., ``rmse_prod``)
best_model_metric.title: Best Model Metric

grad_clip_norm.description: Maximum gradient norm value, by default inf (no clipping)
grad_clip_norm.title: Grad Clip Norm

loss.description:
This section describes the loss function to be used. See the
:ref:`loss-functions` for more details.
loss.title: Loss

batch_atom_bounds.description:
Bounds for the number of atoms per batch as [min, max]. Batches with atom
counts outside these bounds will be skipped during training. Use ``None`` for
either value to disable that bound. This is useful for preventing out-of-memory
errors and ensuring consistent computational load. Default: ``[None, None]``.
batch_atom_bounds.title: Batch Atom Bounds

finetune.description:
Finetuning parameters for MetaMACE models that have been pretrained.

See :ref:`fine-tuning` for more details.

num_tensor_products.description: Number of tensor products per GNN layer.
num_tensor_products.title: Num Tensor Products

num_gnn_layers.description:
Number of GNN layers.

Increasing this value might increase the accuracy of the model (especially on
larger datasets), at the expense of computational efficiency.
num_gnn_layers.title: Num Gnn Layers

cutoff.description:
Cutoff radius for neighbor search.

This should be set to a value after which most of the interactions
between atoms is expected to be negligible. A lower cutoff will lead
to faster models.
cutoff.title: Cutoff

num_neighbors_adaptive.description:
Target number of neighbors for the adaptive cutoff scheme.

This parameter activates the adaptive cutoff functionality.
Each atomic environment has a different cutoff, that is chosen
such that the number of neighbors is approximately equal to this
value. This can be useful to have a more uniform number of neighbors
per atom, especially in sparse systems. Setting it to None disables
this feature and uses all neighbors within the fixed cutoff radius.
num_neighbors_adaptive.title: Num Neighbors Adaptive

cutoff_width.description: Width of the cutoff smoothing function.
cutoff_width.title: Cutoff Width

num_element_channels.description:
Number of channels per element.

This determines the size of the embedding used to encode the atomic species, and it
increases or decreases the size of the internal features used in the model.
num_element_channels.title: Num Element Channels

force_rectangular.description:
Makes the number of channels per irrep the same.

This might improve accuracy with a limited increase in computational cost.
force_rectangular.title: Force Rectangular

radial_basis.description:
Hyperparameters for the radial basis functions.

Raising``max_eigenvalue`` from its default will increase the number of spherical
irreducible representations (irreps) used in the model, which can improve accuracy
at the cost of computational efficiency. Increasing this value will also increase
the number of radial basis functions (and therefore internal features) used for each
irrep.

final_scaling.description: Final scaling factor applied to the model outputs.
final_scaling.title: Final Scaling

use_sphericart.description: Whether to use spherical Cartesian coordinates.
use_sphericart.title: Use Sphericart

mlp_head_num_layers.description: Number of layers in the heads for MLP heads.
mlp_head_num_layers.title: Mlp Head Num Layers

mlp_head_expansion_ratio.description: Expansion ratio for the hidden layers of the MLP head.
mlp_head_expansion_ratio.title: Mlp Head Expansion Ratio

tensor_product_expansion_ratio.description: Expansion ratio for the tensor product iterations.
tensor_product_expansion_ratio.title: Tensor Product Expansion Ratio

heads.description: Heads to use in the model, with options being "linear" or "mlp".
heads.title: Heads

zbl.description: Whether to use the ZBL potential in the model.
zbl.title: Zbl

metatrain__experimental__phace__document.description: Hyperparameters for training the experimental.phace model.

compile.description:
Whether to use `torch.compile` during training.

This can lead to significant speedups, but it will cause a compilation step at the
beginning of training which might take up to 5-10 minutes, mainly depending on
``max_eigenvalue``. Note that this option does not work at the moment with adaptive
cutoffs.
compile.title: Compile

batch_size.description:
Batch size for training.

Decrease this value if you run into out-of-memory errors during training. You can
try to increase it if your structures are very small (less than 20 atoms) and you
have a good GPU.
batch_size.title: Batch Size

num_epochs.description:
Number of epochs to train the model.

A larger number of epochs might lead to better accuracy. In general, if you see
that the validation metrics are not much worse than the training ones at the end of
training, it might be a good idea to increase this value.
num_epochs.title: Num Epochs

learning_rate.description:
Learning rate for the optimizer.

You can try to increase this value (e.g., to 0.01) if training is stable and
slow or decrease it (e.g., to 0.001 or less) if you see divergence in the first
few epochs and/or instabilities.
learning_rate.title: Learning Rate

warmup_fraction.description: Fraction of training steps for learning rate warmup.
warmup_fraction.title: Warmup Fraction

gradient_clipping.description: Gradient clipping value. If None, no clipping is applied.
gradient_clipping.title: Gradient Clipping

ema_decay.description:
Decay factor for exponential moving average of model parameters.
If None, EMA is not used.
ema_decay.title: Ema Decay

log_interval.description: Interval to log metrics during training.
log_interval.title: Log Interval

scale_targets.description:
Normalize targets to unit std during training.

If true, a single scale is computed for each target, given by the uncentered
standard deviation across all values in the dataset for that target.

For targets with more than one property (i.e. > 1 block or >= 1 block with > 1
property), per-property scales are also computed, and used to re-scale model
predictions.

See also :ref:`scale-targets`.
scale_targets.title: Scale Targets

atomic_baseline.description:
The baselines for each target.

By default, ``metatrain`` will fit a linear model (:class:`CompositionModel
<metatrain.utils.additive.composition.CompositionModel>`) to compute the
least squares baseline for each atomic species for each target.

However, this hyperparameter allows you to provide your own baselines.
The value of the hyperparameter should be a dictionary where the keys are the
target names, and the values are either (1) a single baseline to be used for
all atomic types, or (2) a dictionary mapping atomic types to their baselines.
For example:

- ``atomic_baseline: {"energy": {1: -0.5, 6: -10.0}}`` will fix the energy
  baseline for hydrogen (Z=1) to -0.5 and for carbon (Z=6) to -10.0, while
  fitting the baselines for the energy of all other atomic types, as well
  as fitting the baselines for all other targets.
- ``atomic_baseline: {"energy": -5.0}`` will fix the energy baseline for
  all atomic types to -5.0.
- ``atomic_baseline: {"mtt:dos": 0.0}`` sets the baseline for the "mtt:dos"
  target to 0.0, effectively disabling the atomic baseline for that target.

This atomic baseline is substracted from the targets during training, which
avoids the main model needing to learn atomic contributions, and likely makes
training easier. When the model is used in evaluation mode, the atomic baseline
is added on top of the model predictions automatically.

.. note::
    This atomic baseline is a per-atom contribution. Therefore, if the property
    you are predicting is a sum over all atoms (e.g., total energy), the
    contribution of the atomic baseline to the total property will be the
    atomic baseline multiplied by the number of atoms of that type in the
    structure.

.. note::
    If a MACE model is loaded through the ``mace_model`` hyperparameter, the
    atomic baselines in the MACE model are used by default for the target
    indicated in ``mace_head_target``. If you want to override them, you need
    to set explicitly the baselines for that target in this hyperparameter.
atomic_baseline.title: Atomic Baseline

fixed_scaling_weights.description: Fixed scaling weights for the model.
fixed_scaling_weights.title: Fixed Scaling Weights

num_workers.description: Number of workers for data loading.
num_workers.title: Num Workers

per_structure_targets.description: List of targets to calculate per-structure losses.
per_structure_targets.title: Per Structure Targets

log_separate_blocks.description: Whether to log per-block error during training.
log_separate_blocks.title: Log Separate Blocks

log_mae.description: Whether to log MAE alongside RMSE during training.
log_mae.title: Log Mae

best_model_metric.description: Metric used to select the best model checkpoint.
best_model_metric.title: Best Model Metric

metatrain__experimental__phace__document.title: TrainerHypers

regularizer.description:
Value of the regularizer for the energy. It should
be tuned depending on the specific dataset. If it is too small might
lead to overfitting, if it is too big might lead to bad accuracy.
regularizer.title: Regularizer

regularizer_forces.description:
Value of the regularizer for the forces. It has
a similar effect as ``regularizer``. If ``None``, it is set equal to
``regularizer``. It might be changed to have better accuracy.
regularizer_forces.title: Regularizer Forces

num_ensemble_members.description:
Number of ensemble members for each target property for which LLPR ensembles
should be generated. No ensembles will be generated for targets which are not
listed.
num_ensemble_members.title: Num Ensemble Members

distributed_port.description: Port for distributed communication among processes
distributed_port.title: Distributed Port

batch_size.description:
This defines the batch size used in the computation of last-layer
features, covariance matrix, etc.
batch_size.title: Batch Size

regularizer.description:
This is the regularizer value :math:`\varsigma` that is used in
applying Eq. 24 of Bigi et al :footcite:p:`bigi_mlst_2024`:

.. math::

    \sigma^2_\star = \alpha^2 \boldsymbol{\mathrm{f}}^{\mathrm{T}}_\star
    (\boldsymbol{\mathrm{F}}^{\mathrm{T}} \boldsymbol{\mathrm{F}} + \varsigma^2
    \boldsymbol{\mathrm{I}})^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mathrm{f}}_\star

If set to ``null``, the internal routine will determine the smallest regularizer
value that guarantees numerical stability in matrix inversion. Having exposed the
formula here, we also note to the user that the training routine of the LLPR
wrapper model finds the ideal global calibration factor :math:`\alpha`.
regularizer.title: Regularizer

model_checkpoint.description:
This should provide the checkpoint to the model for which the
user wants to perform UQ based on the LLPR approach. Note that the model
architecture must comply with the requirement that the last-layer features are
exposed under the convention defined by metatrain.
model_checkpoint.title: Model Checkpoint

loss.description:
This section describes the loss function to be used during LLPR ensemble
weight training. We strongly suggest only using ensemble-specific loss functions,
i.e. one of "gaussian_nll_ensemble", "gaussian_crps_ensemble",
"empirical_crps_ensemble".
Please refer to the :ref:`loss-functions` documentation for more details of the rest
of the hypers.
loss.title: Loss

num_epochs.description:
Number of epochs for which the LLPR ensemble weight training should
take place. If set to ``null``, only the LLPR covariance matrix computation
and calibration will be performed, without ensemble weight training.
num_epochs.title: Num Epochs

train_all_parameters.description:
Whether to train all parameters of the LLPR-wrapped model, or only the
ensemble weights. If ``true``, all parameters will be trained, including those
of the base model. If ``false``, only the last-layer ensemble weights will be
trained. Note that training all parameters (i.e., setting this flag to ``true``)
will potentially change the uncertainty estimates given by the LLPR through the
``uncertainty`` outputs (because the last-layer features will change).
In that case, only uncertainties calculated as standard deviations over the ensemble
members (``ensemble`` outputs) will be meaningful.
train_all_parameters.title: Train All Parameters

warmup_fraction.description: Fraction of training steps used for learning rate warmup.
warmup_fraction.title: Warmup Fraction

per_structure_targets.description: Targets to calculate per-structure losses and errors on.
per_structure_targets.title: Per Structure Targets

num_workers.description:
Number of workers for data loading. If not provided, it is set
automatically.
num_workers.title: Num Workers

best_model_metric.description: Metric used to select best checkpoint (e.g., ``rmse_prod``)
best_model_metric.title: Best Model Metric

grad_clip_norm.description: Maximum gradient norm value, by default inf (no clipping)
grad_clip_norm.title: Grad Clip Norm

batch_atom_bounds.description:
Bounds for the number of atoms per batch as [min, max]. Batches with atom
counts outside these bounds will be skipped during training. Use ``None`` for
either value to disable that bound. This is useful for preventing out-of-memory
errors and ensuring consistent computational load. Default: ``[None, None]``.
batch_atom_bounds.title: Batch Atom Bounds

calibration_method.description:
This determines how to calculate the calibration factor :math:`\alpha` in
Eq. 24 of Bigi et al :footcite:p:`bigi_mlst_2024`:

.. math::

    \sigma^2_\star = \alpha^2 \boldsymbol{\mathrm{f}}^{\mathrm{T}}_\star
    (\boldsymbol{\mathrm{F}}^{\mathrm{T}} \boldsymbol{\mathrm{F}} + \varsigma^2
    \boldsymbol{\mathrm{I}})^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mathrm{f}}_\star

In any case, a Gaussian error distribution is assumed. If set to
``squared_residuals``, the calibration factor is computed minimizing the negative
log-likelihood. If set to ``absolute_residuals``, the calibration factor is computed
from mean absolute error assuming Gaussian errors. The latter choice is more robust
to outliers and we recommend using it for large and/or uncurated datasets.
If set to ``crps``, continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) is minimized to find
the calibration factor. You might want to use this option if you then want to train
the ensemble weights using a CRPS loss.
calibration_method.title: Calibration Method

cutoff.description:
Cutoff radius for neighbor search.

This should be set to a value after which most of the interactions
between atoms is expected to be negligible. A lower cutoff will lead
to faster models.
cutoff.title: Cutoff

num_neighbors_adaptive.description:
Target number of neighbors for the adaptive cutoff scheme.

This parameter activates the adaptive cutoff functionality.
Each atomic environments has a different cutoff, that is chosen
such that the number of neighbors is approximately equal to this
value. This can be useful to have a more uniform number of neighbors
per atom, especially in sparse systems. Setting it to None disables
this feature and uses all neighbors within the fixed cutoff radius.
num_neighbors_adaptive.title: Num Neighbors Adaptive

adaptive_cutoff_method.description:
Algorithm used to compute the per-atom adaptive cutoffs.

``"grid"`` evaluates the smoothed neighbor count on a discrete probe-cutoff
grid and returns a Gaussian-weighted average of the probes (legacy
behaviour). ``"solver"`` solves ``n_total(r) = num_neighbors_adaptive`` via
a Newton-bisection root finder (default; faster and more accurate). Only
has effect when ``num_neighbors_adaptive`` is set.
adaptive_cutoff_method.title: Adaptive Cutoff Method

cutoff_function.description: Type of the smoothing function at the cutoff
cutoff_function.title: Cutoff Function

cutoff_width.description: Width of the smoothing function at the cutoff
cutoff_width.title: Cutoff Width

cutoff_width_adaptive.description:
Width of the smooth cutoff taper used by the adaptive cutoff scheme.

This controls the taper width of the smoothed neighbor count used to
compute the per-atom adaptive cutoffs. Only has effect when
``num_neighbors_adaptive`` is set.
cutoff_width_adaptive.title: Cutoff Width Adaptive

d_pet.description:
Dimension of the edge features.

This hyperparameters controls width of the neural network. In general,
increasing it might lead to better accuracy, especially on larger datasets, at the
cost of increased training and evaluation time.
d_pet.title: D Pet

d_node.description:
Dimension of the node features.

Increasing this hyperparameter might lead to better accuracy,
with a relatively small increase in inference time.
d_node.title: D Node

d_feedforward.description: Dimension of the feedforward network in the attention layer.
d_feedforward.title: D Feedforward

num_attention_layers.description:
The number of attention layers in each layer of the graph
neural network. Depending on the dataset, increasing this hyperparameter might
lead to better accuracy, at the cost of increased training and evaluation time.
num_attention_layers.title: Num Attention Layers

num_gnn_layers.description:
The number of graph neural network layers.

In general, decreasing this hyperparameter to 1 will lead to much faster models,
at the expense of accuracy. Increasing it may or may not lead to better accuracy,
depending on the dataset, at the cost of increased training and evaluation time.
num_gnn_layers.title: Num Gnn Layers

attention_temperature.description: The temperature scaling factor for attention scores.
attention_temperature.title: Attention Temperature

transformer_type.description:
The order in which the layer normalization and attention
are applied in a transformer block. Available options are ``PreLN``
(normalization before attention) and ``PostLN`` (normalization after attention).
transformer_type.title: Transformer Type

featurizer_type.description:
Implementation of the featurizer of the model to use. Available
options are ``residual`` (the original featurizer from the PET paper, that uses
residual connections at each GNN layer for readout) and ``feedforward`` (a modern
version that uses the last representation after all GNN iterations for readout).
Additionally, the feedforward version uses bidirectional features flow during the
message passing iterations, that favors features flowing from atom ``i`` to atom
``j`` to be not equal to the features flowing from atom ``j`` to atom ``i``.
featurizer_type.title: Featurizer Type

zbl.description: Use ZBL potential for short-range repulsion
zbl.title: Zbl

distributed_port.description: Port for distributed communication among processes
distributed_port.title: Distributed Port

batch_size.description:
The number of samples to use in each batch of training. This
hyperparameter controls the tradeoff between training speed and memory usage. In
general, larger batch sizes will lead to faster training, but might require more
memory.
batch_size.title: Batch Size

warmup_fraction.description: Fraction of training steps used for learning rate warmup.
warmup_fraction.title: Warmup Fraction

atomic_baseline.description:
The baselines for each target.

By default, ``metatrain`` will fit a linear model (:class:`CompositionModel
<metatrain.utils.additive.composition.CompositionModel>`) to compute the
least squares baseline for each atomic species for each target.

However, this hyperparameter allows you to provide your own baselines.
The value of the hyperparameter should be a dictionary where the keys are the
target names, and the values are either (1) a single baseline to be used for
all atomic types, or (2) a dictionary mapping atomic types to their baselines.
For example:

- ``atomic_baseline: {"energy": {1: -0.5, 6: -10.0}}`` will fix the energy
  baseline for hydrogen (Z=1) to -0.5 and for carbon (Z=6) to -10.0, while
  fitting the baselines for the energy of all other atomic types, as well
  as fitting the baselines for all other targets.
- ``atomic_baseline: {"energy": -5.0}`` will fix the energy baseline for
  all atomic types to -5.0.
- ``atomic_baseline: {"mtt:dos": 0.0}`` sets the baseline for the "mtt:dos"
  target to 0.0, effectively disabling the atomic baseline for that target.

This atomic baseline is substracted from the targets during training, which
avoids the main model needing to learn atomic contributions, and likely makes
training easier. When the model is used in evaluation mode, the atomic baseline
is added on top of the model predictions automatically.

.. note::

    This atomic baseline is a per-atom contribution. Therefore, if the property
    you are predicting is a sum over all atoms (e.g., total energy), the
    contribution of the atomic baseline to the total property will be the
    atomic baseline multiplied by the number of atoms of that type in the
    structure.
atomic_baseline.title: Atomic Baseline

scale_targets.description:
Normalize targets to unit std during training.

If true, a single scale is computed for each target, given by the uncentered
standard deviation across all values in the dataset for that target.

For targets with more than one property (i.e. > 1 block or >= 1 block with > 1
property), per-property scales are also computed, and used to re-scale model
predictions.

See also :ref:`scale-targets`.
scale_targets.title: Scale Targets

fixed_scaling_weights.description:
Weights for target scaling.

This is passed to the ``fixed_weights`` argument of
:meth:`Scaler.train_model <metatrain.utils.scaler.scaler.Scaler.train_model>`,
see its documentation to understand exactly what to pass here.
fixed_scaling_weights.title: Fixed Scaling Weights

per_structure_targets.description: Targets to calculate per-structure losses and errors on.
per_structure_targets.title: Per Structure Targets

num_workers.description:
Number of workers for data loading. If not provided, it is set
automatically.
num_workers.title: Num Workers

best_model_metric.description: Metric used to select best checkpoint (e.g., ``rmse_prod``)
best_model_metric.title: Best Model Metric

loss.description:
This section describes the loss function to be used. See the
:ref:`loss-functions` for more details.
loss.title: Loss

batch_atom_bounds.description:
Bounds for the number of atoms per batch as [min, max]. Batches with atom
counts outside these bounds will be skipped during training. Use ``None`` for
either value to disable that bound. This is useful for preventing out-of-memory
errors and ensuring consistent computational load. Default: ``[None, None]``.
batch_atom_bounds.title: Batch Atom Bounds

max_atoms_per_batch.description:
If set, use greedy atom-count packing instead of fixed ``batch_size``.
Structures are accumulated into each batch until adding another would exceed this
limit, producing variable numbers of structures per batch. Only supported with
``MemmapDataset``. When set, ``batch_size`` is ignored for constructing training
and validation batches (it is still used internally for composition model and
scaler fitting). ``batch_atom_bounds`` filtering in the collate function is also
disabled, as atom-count bounds are enforced at packing time instead.
max_atoms_per_batch.title: Max Atoms Per Batch

min_atoms_per_batch.description:
Minimum total number of atoms required to keep a batch when
``max_atoms_per_batch`` is set. Batches whose total atom count falls below this
threshold are discarded during packing. Defaults to ``0`` (no minimum).
min_atoms_per_batch.title: Min Atoms Per Batch

finetune.description:
Parameters for fine-tuning trained PET models.

See :ref:`label_fine_tuning_concept` for more details.
finetune.title: Finetune

metatrain__soap_bpnn__documentation__Mod.description: Hyperparameters for the SOAP + BPNN architecture.

legacy.description:
If true, uses the legacy implementation without chemical embedding and with one
MLP head per atomic species.
legacy.title: Legacy

bpnn.description: Configuration of the neural network architecture.

add_lambda_basis.description:
This boolean parameter controls whether or not to add a spherical
expansion term of the same angular order as the targets, when they are
tensorial.
add_lambda_basis.title: Add Lambda Basis

heads.description:
The type of head (“linear” or “mlp”) to use for each target
(e.g. heads: {"energy": "linear", "mtt::dipole": "mlp"}). All omitted
targets will use a MLP (multi-layer perceptron) head. MLP heads consists
of one hidden layer with as many neurons as the
SOAP-BPNN (see ``BPNNConfig.num_neurons_per_layer``).
heads.title: Heads

zbl.description:
Whether to use the ZBL short-range repulsion as the baseline for the model.
May be needed to achieve better description at the close-contact, repulsive regime.
zbl.title: Zbl

long_range.description:
Parameters related to long-range interactions.

May be needed to describe important long-range effects not captured by
the short-range SOAP-BPNN model

metatrain__soap_bpnn__documentation__Mod.title: ModelHypers

metatrain__soap_bpnn__documentation__Tra.description: Hyperparameters for training SOAP BPNN models.

distributed_port.description: Port for distributed communication among processes
distributed_port.title: Distributed Port

batch_size.description:
The number of samples to use in each batch of training. This
hyperparameter controls the tradeoff between training speed and memory usage. In
general, larger batch sizes will lead to faster training, but might require more
memory.
batch_size.title: Batch Size

warmup_fraction.description: Fraction of training steps used for learning rate warmup.
warmup_fraction.title: Warmup Fraction

However, this hyperparameter allows you to provide your own baselines.
The value of the hyperparameter should be a dictionary where the keys are the

For example:

- ``atomic_baseline: {"energy": {1: -0.5, 6: -10.0}}`` will fix the energy

- ``atomic_baseline: {"energy": -5.0}`` will fix the energy baseline for

- ``atomic_baseline: {"mtt:dos": 0.0}`` sets the baseline for the "mtt:dos"

This atomic baseline is substracted from the targets during training, which

.. note::

    This atomic baseline is a per-atom contribution. Therefore, if the property

atomic_baseline.title: Atomic Baseline

Normalize targets to unit std during training.

If true, a single scale is computed for each target, given by the uncentered

For targets with more than one property (i.e. > 1 block or >= 1 block with > 1

scale_targets.title: Scale Targets

fixed_scaling_weights.description:
Weights for target scaling.

This is passed to the ``fixed_weights`` argument of

fixed_scaling_weights.title: Fixed Scaling Weights

per_structure_targets.description: Targets to calculate per-structure losses and errors on.

num_workers.description:
Number of workers for data loading. If not provided, it is set
automatically.
num_workers.title: Num Workers

best_model_metric.description: Metric used to select best checkpoint (e.g., ``rmse_prod``)
best_model_metric.title: Best Model Metric

loss.description:
This section describes the loss function to be used. See the
:ref:`loss-functions` for more details.
loss.title: Loss

Bounds for the number of atoms per batch as [min, max]. Batches with atom

batch_atom_bounds.title: Batch Atom Bounds
